nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing rivers and tributaries with rocky substrates, sandy or gravelly beds, and clear, well-oxygenated water; found in the Mekong and Chao Phraya river systems

decorPreferences: fine sand or smooth gravel substrate
dense planting along tank edges with open swimming space
PVC pipes or ceramic tubes as bolt-holes

diet.notes: Accepts a wide range of foods including sinking pellets, wafers, frozen or live bloodworms, brine shrimp, daphnia, tubifex, and blanched vegetables (zucchini, cucumber, spinach). Will actively hunt and consume snails, making it an effective biological control for pest snail populations. Feed primarily in the evening as this species becomes most active around dusk.

goodWith: robust mid-water schooling fish (e.g., giant danios, larger barbs)
avoidWith: slow-moving long-finned fish (betta, angelfish, fancy guppies — fin-nipping risk)
timid or sedentary bottom-dwellers that will be outcompeted
small peaceful community fish that may be harassed
snails intended to be kept (will be eaten)
dwarf shrimp (likely to be eaten or stressed)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Yasuhikotakia morleti; verify current availability at fishbase.se summary page.

summary: A spirited Southeast Asian loach from the Mekong and Chao Phraya drainages, the Skunk Loach is named for the bold dark stripe running along its dorsal midline. Notorious fin-nippers despite their loach classification; best kept in groups of 5+ with robust, short-finned tankmates. Copper-sensitive.
careNotes:
The Skunk Loach (Yasuhikotakia morleti) was reclassified from Botia morleti into the genus Yasuhikotakia by Kottelat in 2004, a split that separated several Southeast Asian loach species from the traditional Botia genus. It is native to the Mekong and Chao Phraya river systems of Thailand, Laos, Cambodia, and Vietnam, where it inhabits clear, well-oxygenated rivers with rocky and gravelly substrates.

Despite being a loach — a group generally associated with peaceful or semi-peaceful behavior — the Skunk Loach has a well-documented reputation as an aggressive fin-nipper. Slow-moving or long-finned tankmates such as bettas, angelfish, and fancy guppies are highly vulnerable and should not be kept with this species. Select robust, active, short-finned companions instead.

Shoaling is essential: single specimens or pairs become far more aggressive and may relentlessly harass tankmates. A group of at least 5 distributes intraspecific aggression and produces noticeably calmer, more natural behavior. Provide ample caves, pipes, and shaded retreats so each fish can claim a bolt-hole.

This species is highly sensitive to copper-based medications — a common treatment for ich and other parasites. Never use copper treatments in a tank containing Skunk Loaches. Alternatives such as heat treatment, formalin, or ich-specific copper-free medications should be used instead. As scaleless or thin-scaled loaches, they are also sensitive to many other chemical treatments and require half-doses of any medication as a precaution.

Water quality must be excellent: the Skunk Loach is intolerant of ammonia, nitrite, and elevated nitrate. A strong filter with good oxygenation is recommended, replicating the well-aerated rivers of its native range. Cover the tank securely, as loaches are accomplished escape artists. Feed primarily in the evening when activity peaks, and include snail-hunting sessions — this species is an effective biological control for pest snail infestations.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been reliably achieved by hobbyists. In the wild, spawning is believed to occur during monsoon season when water temperatures drop and flow increases. Commercial specimens are likely wild-caught or produced via hormonal induction at fish farms in Southeast Asia. No practical captive-breeding protocol is established for home aquaria.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Yasuhikotakia morleti
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (~10 cm), temperature 24–30°C, pH 6.5–7.5, hardness 5–15 dH, native range (Mekong and Chao Phraya basins), biotope, diet, conspecific behavior, fin-nipping advisory, copper sensitivity, and taxonomy notes including Kottelat 2004 reclassification.

additional.name: FishBase — Yasuhikotakia morleti
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 10 cm TL), taxonomy (Botiidae, Cypriniformes), native range countries (Thailand, Laos, Cambodia, Vietnam), diet (invertivore/omnivore), and habitat description. Confirmed Yasuhikotakia genus placement per Kottelat 2004.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Skunk Loach Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade care guide; confirmed minimum group size of 5+, fin-nipping behavior toward slow-moving tankmates, copper sensitivity, recommended tank size (minimum 30 gallons / ~114 L for a group), diet preferences including snail hunting, and bottom-dwelling swim zone.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Botia morleti profile
additional.notes: Species profile under legacy name Botia morleti; confirmed Southeast Asian origin, semi-aggressive nature, loach family characteristics, shoaling requirement, and general water parameter ranges consistent with primary source.

fish.breedingNotes: No established captive-breeding protocol for home aquaria. Wild spawning linked to monsoon-season triggers (temperature drop, increased flow). Commercial supply likely wild-caught or hormone-induced farm production.
